data.title: UK's Wickes jumps on share buyback, trading update
data.description:
** British home improvement retailer Wickes up as much as 8% to 186p; among top pct gainers on FTSE small cap index (.FTSC)

** Says trading in the 11 weeks of 2025 in line with expectations on higher LFL sales in its retail segment

** Posts adjusted PBT of 43.6 mln pounds ($56.6 mln), beating midpoint of company-compiled analysts' estimate of 43.2 mln pounds

** Announces further 20 million pound share buyback

** "The group remains a coiled spring, ready to capitalise on better market volumes with significant operational gearing and strong cash conversion," analysts at Peel Hunt say

** WIX up ~20% YTD

($1 = 0.7709 pounds)

data.title: XRP Price Slips. Why the Crypto Is Falling Despite Its SEC Win. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By George Glover

XRP was giving up some of its gains Thursday, as investors took the opportunity to lock in profit following a big rally.

The token, which is used to settle and facilitate transactions on Ripple Labs' digital-payments platform, slipped 1.7% to $2.45, according to data from the crypto exchange Kraken.

XRP had jumped about 9% on Wednesday after Ripple CEO Brad Garlinghouse said in a post on X that the Securities and Exchange Commission had dropped its case against the company, bringing an end to a legal battle that had dragged on for more than four years. When reached by Barron's, the SEC declined to comment.

XRP was likely sliding Thursday because some traders saw Ripple's apparent win over the agency as a good time to take profit. Now that the market finally has some clarity on that front, the next question will be if another catalyst emerges that can drive the token even higher.

Write to George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Swiss Franc Falls After SNB Cuts Rates — Market Talk
data.description:
0849 GMT - The Swiss franc falls after the Swiss National Bank cut interest rates by 25 basis points to 0.25%. Markets were pricing in a 65% chance of such a move, according to LSEG. The SNB pointed to low inflationary pressures and "heightened downside risks" to inflation. It said it would continue to monitor the situation closely and adjust its monetary policy if necessary to ensure price stability. The SNB noted solid economic growth in Switzerland in the fourth quarter of 2024 but said the outlook has become more uncertain given risks from global trade and geopolitical tensions. The euro rises to 0.9584 francs after the decision, from 0.9536 beforehand. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0831 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, fall following U.K. labor market data and ahead of a Bank of England decision at 1200 GMT where interest rates are expected to remain on hold at 4.5%. U.K. average wage growth excluding bonuses in the three months to January was steady at 5.9% and the unemployment rate remained unchanged at 4.4% in the same period. Despite resilient wage growth data, however, "there are now some cracks appearing," Pantheon Macroeconomics' Elliott Jordan-Doak says in a note. He cites in particular the rise in jobless claims from 22,000 to 44,200,The 10-year gilt yield falls 3 basis points to 4.601%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0828 GMT - Bitcoin rises after the Federal Reserve downplayed the risk of higher inflation resulting from U.S. tariffs. Alongside the Fed's decision to leave interest rates unchanged Wednesday, Fed Chair Jerome Powell said the potential impact of U.S. tariffs on inflation would likely be temporary. The Fed will probably cut rates in response to signs of weakness in the labor market even if inflation remains around its current level, UBS Global Wealth Management analysts say in note. "We continue to expect two 25 basis points interest-rate cuts later this year." Bitcoin rises 0.5% to $85,806 after hitting a near two-week high of $87,462 overnight, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0811 GMT - The Riksbank's policy decision due at 0830 GMT is unlikely to materially move the Swedish krona, Commerzbank analyst Antje Praefcke says in a note. The Riksbank is expected to keep interest rates unchanged, although it could leave the door open to further cuts given the krona's recent strength, she says. "The interest rate meeting should not bring any major surprises, so it should be neutral for the krona." The euro falls 0.1% to 11.0097 krona ahead of the decision. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0807 GMT - The Swiss franc could weaken if the Swiss National Bank cuts interest rates by another 25 basis points in a decision at 0830 GMT, Commerzbank analyst Michael Pfister says in a note. The market is pricing in a 65% chance of a rate cut, according to LSEG. Given the market is not fully pricing in such a move, the franc is likely to "suffer somewhat," Pfister says. "In the medium-term, however, what matters is what the SNB signals for coming months." The extent of the franc's fall will depend on whether the SNB lowers its inflation forecasts and hints at further cuts, he says. Ahead of the decision, the euro falls 0.3% to 0.9541 francs. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0804 GMT - U.K. labor market data remains resilient even as labor costs are set to go higher starting in April, Quilter Investors investment strategist Lindsay James says in a note. The unemployment rate was unchanged at 4.4% in the three months to January, and average earnings excluding bonuses in the three months to January remained steady at 5.9%. However, the number of vacancies is likely to fall as costs go up. "Vacancies remain above pre-pandemic levels, but it is worth noting that there has been a consistent decline and we could see this pick up as businesses contend with higher costs," James says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0752 GMT - The dollar rises as investors continue to digest the Federal Reserve's latest policy decision. The Fed left rates unchanged as expected Wednesday, but signalled it would continue to cut rates to support the economy. The dollar initially fell after the decision. However, Swissquote Bank analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya says the prospect of rate cuts which would reduce the chances of recession could support the dollar. "Right now the currency pricing is influenced by growth expectations." The Fed downplayed the potential impact of tariffs on inflation while cutting its growth forecast, she says. The DXY dollar index rises 0.2% to 103.642. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0736 GMT - Sterling is little changed after data showed the U.K. unemployment rate remained unchanged and wage growth steadied in the three months to January, as expected. The unemployment rate held at 4.4%. Average earnings excluding bonuses rose 5.9%, in line with the previous three-month period's growth. The data comes ahead of the Bank of England's interest rate decision at 1200 GMT. "There is nothing in this data that will shift the BOE away from its current thinking about the economy, with interest rates almost certainly set to remain unchanged today," Aberdeen economist Luke Bartholomew says in note. Sterling is last down 0.2% at $1.2982, compared with $1.2984 before the data. The euro trades flat at 0.8388, versus 0.8389 beforehand. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0723 GMT - German Bunds have the potential to firm on Thursday amid a busy day in the primary market with government bond issuance in Spain, France and Ireland, says Commerzbank Research's Hauke Siemssen in a note. "We see potential for Bunds longs," the rates strategist says. Drivers could be a likely interest-rate cut by the Swiss National Bank and potentially dovish comments by European Central Bank officials, he says. "Taken together, an SNB cut and dovish ECB remarks argue for Bunds to trade better supported." ECB President Christine Lagarde will be heard at the EU parliament's committee, while ECB chief economist Philip Lane will give a lecture "where the usual dovish undertone can be expected," he says. The 10-year Bund yield falls 3 basis points to 2.77%, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0658 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields fall slightly in early European trading after the Federal Reserve's pause. Fed officials are facing the challenge of balancing rising inflation and recession risks that appear to be increasing in tandem, Pimco's Tiffany Wilding says in a note. While Fed Chair Jerome Powell signaled that officials are comfortable keeping rates on hold in the near term, "we think unemployment will be the ultimate arbiter," the economist says. Pimco still expects the Fed to cut aggressively if the unemployment rate starts to move higher, she says. The two-year Treasury yield eases 0.5 basis point to 3.974%, and the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield declines 2 basis points to 4.237%, according to Tradeweb data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0647 GMT - The Federal Reserve's statement and updated dot plot underscore the data volatility and uncertainty the U.S. central bank faces in resuming its cutting cycle, says IFM Investors' ​Ryan Weldon in a note. "While the jobs market has stabilized and inflation appears to be slowly trending towards its target, the Fed will need several months of consistent data to convince them to cut their policy rate again," the investment director says. The Fed will need to balance its decision-making around its sharply reduced economic growth projections and its expectation for higher inflation in 2025, says Weldon, who is also a portfolio manager. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0641 GMT - The Federal Reserve's reaction function is really complicated, says Global X's Scott Helfstein. The Fed extended its pause on interest rates, as expected, raised its inflation forecasts and lowered growth projections. "They have to grapple with rising risk to both mandates, employment and inflation simultaneously," says the head of investment strategy. This isn't stagflation in the traditional sense, he says, adding that policy is having an outsize impact on consumer and business expectations. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Correction: Zhejiang Vie Science & Technology Ties Up With Hwaway Technology to Set Up Moroccan Joint Venture
data.description:
(Corrected Huawei to Hwaway Technology and included Hwaway's stock code)

Zhejiang Vie Science & Technology partnered with Hwaway Technology to establish a joint venture in Morocco, according to a Shenzhen Stock Exchange disclosure on Wednesday.

The venture will have a registered capital of 100,000 Moroccan dirhams and will produce automotive parts.

The Chinese automobile parts manufacturer will invest 10.5 million euros and Hwaway will inject 19.5 million euros into the venture's capital, owning 35% and 65% of the venture, respectively.

Both Zhejiang Vie's and Hwaway's shares closed less than 3% higher.

data.title: Hong Kong Inflation Eases in February
data.description:
The annual inflation rate in Hong Kong eased to 1.4% in February 2025 from January’s four-month high of 2%.

This was mainly due to softer price increases for food (0.1% vs 1.2% in January), transport (2.6% vs 4%), and miscellaneous services (1.2% vs 2.8%).

Additionally, costs for durable goods decreased (-1.1% vs -1%).

Meanwhile, prices rose for housing (1.6% vs 1.5%), electricity, gas and water (13.3% vs 13.2%), alcoholic beverages and tobacco (20.9% vs 20.7%), and miscellaneous goods (1.4% vs 0.8%).

On a monthly basis, consumer prices fell by 0.1% in February, reversing a 0.4% rise in the previous month.

data.title: Zoomcar to Consolidate Shares in 1-for-20 Reverse Split
data.description:
Zoomcar Holdings said Wednesday it will implement a 1-for-20 reverse stock split, effective Friday.

The company said its shares will start trading on a reverse-split basis on March 24 under the same Nasdaq ticker symbol.

Zoomcar said the move will shrink its outstanding shares from about 14.2 million to roughly 710,000.

The company said the adjustment is meant to lift its share price and support compliance with Nasdaq's listing rules.

Shares of the company were down more than 28% in recent Thursday premarket activity.

data.title: Sodexo Shares Plunge After Profit Warning
data.description:
By Maitane Sardon

Shares in Sodexo fell after the French food-catering company trimmed its full-year profitability guidance amid slower growth in North America.

Shares were 13% lower at 63.15 euros in early European trade Thursday.

Sodexo said it expects organic revenue to increase between 3% and 4% in fiscal 2025, compared with the 5.5% to 6.5% it was initially expecting.

The company said it now sees the underlying operating profit margin increasing by 10 to 20 basis points, down from a previously expected rise of 30 to 40 basis points.

"In North America, the continued soft trend in volumes in education, [the] slower-than-expected net new ramp-up in healthcare have impacted our ability to meet initial expectations," Sodexo Chief Executive Sophie Bellon said.

Sodexo posted revenue of 12.47 billion euros ($13.60 billion) for the first half of the year ended Aug. 31, up 3.1% on year. Underlying operating profit came to 651 million euros, compared with 612 million euros the year prior.

Net profit in the first half was 434 million euros, up from 496 million euros.

Write to Maitane Sardon at maitane.sardon@wsj.com

data.title: French Stocks Hover Flatline
data.description:
The CAC 40 hovered around the flatline at 8,163 on Thursday, following a four-session winning streak, as investors digested a wave of interest rate decisions from major central banks across Europe.

The Swiss National Bank lowered its policy rate by 25 bps to 0.25% in March 2025, as expected, while the Swedish Riksbank held its policy rate steady at 2.25%, in line with expectations.

The Bank of England is also expected to keep interest rates unchanged.

Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve opted to hold rates steady but hinted at potential rate cuts later in the year, despite the ongoing uncertainties surrounding US President Donald Trump’s trade policies.

Among individual stocks, Eurofins Scientific stood out, climbing 5.1% to a one-week high after announcing the launch of its fifth share buy-back program.

Conversely, Stellantis NV led the laggards with shares dropping 1.3%, followed by ArcelorMittal with shares falling 1.2% and LVMH down 1%.

data.title: Market Chatter: Mitsubishi Motors Eyes Foxconn for EV Manufacturing
data.description:
Mitsubishi Motors plans to have Foxconn produce its electric vehicles to cut costs, Kyodo News reported Thursday, citing sources familiar with the matter.

The Japanese automaker, which is allied with Nissan, is expected to confirm the plan with Foxconn, formally Hon Hai Precision Industry Co., in the coming weeks, the report said.

Mitsubishi would sell Foxconn-produced cars under its own brand, but the models are yet to be finalized, according to the report.

Foxconn, which has sought EV tie-ups with Honda and Nissan, is expanding in the competitive EV market.

Mitsubishi, which has a strong presence in Southeast Asia, had considered a merger with Nissan and Honda , but talks collapsed in February.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: CK Hutchison 2024 Net HK$17.09B Vs. Net HK$23.50B >0001.HK
RTL Group Shares Fall After Ad Revenue Decline, Dividend Cut
data.description:
By Adria Calatayud

RTL Group shares fell after the European media group reported a decline in fourth-quarter advertising revenue and cut its dividend.

Shares in RTL fell 9.1% to 32.50 euros in European morning trade, but remained up 22% in the year to date.

The company said its total advertising revenue for the fourth quarter fell 4.7% to 1.00 billion euros ($1.09 billion), while TV advertising revenue dropped 6.4% to 742 million euros.

Overall revenue was broadly stable for the year as a whole at 6.25 billion euros, RTL said. Full-year adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization--the company's preferred metric, which strips out exceptional and other one-off items--was 7.8% lower at 721 million euros.

The company faced challenging market conditions in the second half of the year, but its streaming services and content-production business reached turning points that will pave the way for higher operating profits in the coming years, Chief Executive Thomas Rabe said.

RTL's board proposed a dividend of 2.50 euros a share for 2024, down from 2.75 euros a share a year before.

The company expects 2025 adjusted Ebita and revenue to rise to 780 million euros and 6.45 billion euros, respectively, it said.

Write to Adria Calatayud at adria.calatayud@wsj.com

data.title: China Mobile: Co. Is Presented With Ample Opportunities to Realize Potential>0941.HK
UnionBank Named Best Wealth Management Bank in the Philippines
data.description:
MANILA, Mar 20, 2025 - (ACN Newswire) - Union Bank of the Philippines has been awarded with the Best Wealth Management Bank in the Philippines at the 2025 Global Excellence in Retail Finance Awards, held in February by The Asian Banker. A testament to its strategic vision, innovative wealth solutions, and unwavering commitment to financial excellence, this accolade underscores UnionBank's success in redefining the wealth management landscape through digital innovation, strategic acquisitions, and customer-centric investment solutions.

Setting a New Benchmark in Wealth Management

"UnionBank has strengthened its affluent banking proposition by seamlessly integrating the acquired Citi wealth business, further enhancing its ability to serve a diverse spectrum of wealth clients. From emerging affluent individuals to high-net-worth investors, the bank has introduced a transformative approach to wealth management, providing tailored solutions that cater to clients' evolving financial needs," read The Asian Banker's citation at the Awards, held this year in Tokyo, Japan.

"For its successful integration of acquired expertise, relentless commitment to financial innovation, and dedication to delivering sophisticated wealth solutions, UnionBank is honored with the title of Best Wealth Management Bank in the Philippines. This recognition reflects the bank's mission to empower Filipinos in achieving their financial goals through world-class wealth management services."

Introducing Elite and Access: A New Era in Wealth Solutions

A key milestone in UnionBank's wealth management evolution is the launch of Elite and Access, two pioneering programs designed to provide tiered wealth solutions:

* Access is tailored for emerging affluent clients, offering seamless digital wealth tools and an extensive suite of investment products.

* Elite is designed for high-net-worth investors, delivering bespoke financial planning, exclusive global investment opportunities, and dedicated relationship management.

These programs redefine financial accessibility and personalization, ensuring that clients receive the right level of support, expertise, and product offerings based on their financial standing and aspirations.

"We thank The Asian Banker for recognizing our unwavering commitment to excellence, innovation, and, most importantly, our clients. At UnionBank wealth management is about building trust, securing futures, and creating opportunities," said UnionBank Wealth & Brokerage Head and Board Director of UB Financial Services and Insurance Brokerage Inc. (UFSI), Therese Chan.

"The strategies we craft and solutions we provide are driven by our dedication to helping our clients achieve their financial goals. We remain committed to setting new standards in wealth management and empowering more people to build a stronger financial future," continued Ms Chan.

UnionBank's ability to serve a diverse spectrum of wealth clients is further bolstered by its world-class Wealth Center, which was unveiled to the media on the same day it received the prestigious Asian Banker Award.

Digital Innovation Meets Global Investment Access

UnionBank continues to lead the industry with its enhanced digital wealth platform, allowing clients to diversify their portfolios with global investment products - without the need for offshore accounts. This innovation ensures that clients can securely access a world of investment opportunities from the convenience of a seamless, digital-first banking experience.

By blending leading-edge technology with highly personalized advisory services, UnionBank has solidified its position as the preferred financial partner for wealth clients across the Philippines. The bank's dedication to innovation, accessibility, and global investment integration has set a new gold standard in the country's wealth management sector.

As UnionBank continues to drive financial excellence and innovation, it remains steadfast in its vision to elevate wealth management in the Philippines paving the way for a smarter, more inclusive financial future to help clients build, grow, and protect their wealth.

To learn more about UnionBank Elite, visit www.unionbankph.com/wealth/elite or email ubwealth@unionbankph.com.

Source: Union Bank of the Philippines

Copyright 2025 ACN Newswire . All rights reserved.

data.title: Gehua CATV Network Chief Accountant Resigns
data.description:
Beijing Gehua CATV Network said Hu Zhipeng resigned as chief accountant after reaching the statutory retirement age, according to a Thursday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

Shares of the information technology and services company closed 2% lower Thursday.

data.title: Euro zone inflation could jump, growth sink on trade war with U.S., Lagarde says
data.description:
The euro zone economy would take a large hit from a full-scale trade war with the United States and inflation could surge, ECB President Christine Lagarde said on Thursday, arguing that greater, not less, trade integration was the solution.

The U.S. has already imposed tariffs on steel and aluminium products from around the world and said it would review its trade relationship with the European Union. This has already prompted retaliatory measures from the EU, which are set to come into effect in April.

A 25% tariff imposed by the U.S. on imports from Europe would lower euro zone growth by about 0.3 percentage points in the first year, while retaliatory measures could increase this to about half a percentage point, European Central Bank president Lagarde told EU lawmakers in Brussels.

"In the near term, EU retaliatory measures and a weaker euro exchange rate – resulting from lower U.S. demand for European products – could lift inflation by around half a percentage point," she said. "The effect would ease in the medium term due to lower economic activity dampening inflationary pressures."

Lagarde, however, stressed that any estimate was subject to "considerable" uncertainty.

Nevertheless, the ECB would be "vigilant" and ready to act to protect price stability, even if it remained open-minded about its next move and would take decisions meeting-by-meeting, she added.

The solution for any trade war could be further trade integration with others, which might even offset the negative impact of the U.S. measures, Lagarde said.

"ECB analysis indicates that closer integration with the rest of the world could more than offset losses incurred from unilateral tariffs, including retaliation," Lagarde said.

"In such a scenario only countries engaging in isolationist policies stand to lose.

data.title: Hong Kong Stocks Slide as China, US Maintain Rates; Tech Stocks Plunge
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks fell on Thursday as investor sentiment dampened after China and the US kept interest rates unchanged, despite the US Fed signaling potential rate cuts later in the year.

The Hang Seng Index slid 2.23%, or 551.19 points, at 24,219.95. The Hang Seng China Enterprises index also fell 2.33%, or 213.49 points, at 8,950.18.

Sentiment reflected disappointment over the People's Bank of China decision to keep its one-year loan prime rate (LPR) steady at 3.1% and its five-year LPR at 3.6% for the fifth straight month.

At its latest policy meeting, the US Federal Reserve also kept its rate unchanged at between 4.25% and 4.5%. Investor mood remained downbeat despite a reassurance from Jerome Powell, chair of the US Fed, for potential rate cuts this year, the SCMP reported.

Powell further stated that the effect of Donald Trump's tariff policy on inflation would be "transitionary."

In corporate news, tech giants were among firms leading the decline on Thursday. Alibaba Group , Tencent and Meituan each slumped 4%, with Xiaomi falling 3% at the day end.

data.title: Liquidia Files Secondary Common Stock Shelf
data.description:
Liquidia filed a shelf registration statement with the US Securities And Exchange Commission Wednesday covering the potential resale of up to 1.1 million common shares of the company by certain stockholders from time to time.

The company said it will not receive any proceeds from the sale of the shares as it is not selling the securities.

data.title: Fidelity European Trust Posts Lower FY24 Net Return, Swings to Loss on Investments
data.description:
Fidelity European Trust said Thursday that net return for full-year 2024 tumbled as it swung to a loss on investments.

Net return after tax for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 10.8 million pounds sterling, compared with 238.7 million pounds earlier. Return per share moved to 0.0263 pound from 0.5840 pound.

The investment trust's loss on investments was 47.3 million pounds, compared with a gain of 165.9 million pounds a year ago.

Meanwhile, the board proposed a final dividend of 0.0550 pound per share, payable May 13 to shareholders on record March 28. It paid 0.0499 pound per share a year ago. This takes the full-year dividend to 0.091 pound per share, up from 0.0825 pound per share in the previous year.

data.title: Portugal's Sonae reports record sales in 2024, profitability increase
data.description:
Portugal's Sonae , which owns the country's largest food retailer, on Thursday said sales rose to a record level in 2024 and profitability improved.

The conglomerate, which spans retail and telecoms, announced consolidated net profit of 223 million euros ($243 million), substantially short of the 263 million average forecast by analysts polled by LSEG.

Excluding a 168 million euro capital gain booked in 2023 on the sale of sports goods retailer ISGR, consolidated net profit rose 18% last year.

The group said consolidated sales rose 18.4% to a record 10 billion euros. Analysts expected sales of 9.6 billion euros.

Sales at the food retail unit MC, which runs around 400 Continente hypermarkets and large supermarkets and other chains, increased 15.3% to 7.6 billion euros, with like-for-like sales rising 4.4%.

Chief Executive Claudia Azevedo said that "all businesses performed exceptionally well in a highly competitive context". She attributed sales growth to higher volumes as inflation was low.

Azevedo said she saw opportunities in proximity and convenience stores as well as online sales.

Underlying earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortisation (EBITDA) increased 26% to 908 million euros, while the underlying EBITDA margin - a key measure of profitability - improved to 9.1% from 8.6% a year earlier.

Shares were up 0.6% in morning trading on Thursday.

The board will propose to raise the dividend by 5% to 5.921 euro cents per share.

($1 = 0.9167 euros)
